q: What is Vertex AI, and what problem does a unified ML platform solve over stitching tools together yourself?
a: Vertex AI is Google Cloud's managed platform that unifies the full ML lifecycle — data, training, tuning, serving, MLOps, and generative AI — under one API, IAM model, and metadata store. Instead of gluing together separate notebooks, training VMs, a model registry, a serving stack, and a feature store, you get managed services that share lineage and security. It spans both classic ML (AutoML, custom training) and GenAI (Gemini, Model Garden, grounding, agents). In 2026 Google rebranded the broader suite as the Gemini Enterprise Agent Platform, though most APIs and docs still carry the Vertex AI name. The value is reduced operational glue and consistent governance across the lifecycle.
fj: Vertex AI is just Google's hosted Jupyter notebooks.
fs: Notebooks (Workbench) are one piece — Vertex AI spans training, tuning, serving, registry, monitoring, and GenAI under one platform.

q: What is Model Garden, and how does it relate to Gemini?
a: Model Garden is Vertex AI's curated catalog of 200-plus models you can discover, test, tune, and deploy: Google's own foundation models (Gemini, Imagen for images, Veo for video), partner models like Anthropic's Claude and Mistral, and open models like Llama and Gemma. Gemini is Google's flagship multimodal family within the garden, accessed through the generative AI API rather than a self-managed endpoint. Open models you deploy yourself spin up a real serving endpoint with GPUs you pay for. Model Garden is the entry point for choosing between fully managed Google APIs and self-hosted open weights.
fj: Model Garden only contains Google's own models.
fs: It also hosts third-party (Claude, Mistral) and open models (Llama, Gemma) alongside Google's Gemini, Imagen, and Veo.

q: How do you call a Gemini model on Vertex AI, and what does 'multimodal' actually buy you?
a: You call the generative AI API through the google-genai SDK (or REST), pointing at a Vertex project and region, and send a prompt that can mix text, images, PDFs, audio, or video in one request. Multimodal means a single model reasons across modalities natively — you can pass a chart image plus a question, or a video plus a transcript task, without a separate vision pipeline. Gemini 2.5 Flash and Pro are GA, with the Gemini 3 family in preview offering large (1M-token) context windows. Flash variants trade some quality for much lower latency and cost. You tune behavior with system instructions and generation config rather than retraining.
fj: You need separate models for text, vision, and audio.
fs: Gemini is natively multimodal — one request can mix text, images, audio, and video against one model.

q: When would you use AutoML versus custom training on Vertex AI?
a: AutoML lets you hand Vertex a labeled tabular, image, text, or video dataset and it searches architectures and hyperparameters for you, producing a deployable model with no training code — ideal when you lack ML engineers or need a strong baseline fast. Custom training runs your own container or Python training package on managed compute (CPU, GPU, or TPU), giving full control over framework, architecture, and data handling — needed for novel model types, custom losses, or large-scale deep learning. The trade is AutoML's speed and simplicity versus custom training's flexibility and ceiling. A common pattern is AutoML for a baseline, then custom training only if it justifies the engineering cost.
fj: Custom training is always better, so skip AutoML.
fs: AutoML often gives a strong baseline with zero code — only move to custom training when the flexibility actually pays off.

q: What are Vertex AI Pipelines, and how do they relate to Kubeflow?
a: Vertex AI Pipelines is a managed, serverless orchestrator for ML workflows built on Kubeflow Pipelines (KFP); you define a DAG of containerized components with the KFP SDK (or TFX) and submit it, and Google runs each step on managed infra. You pay per pipeline run with no cluster to operate, unlike self-hosting Kubeflow on GKE. Each run integrates with Vertex ML Metadata, automatically recording artifacts, executions, and lineage so you can trace which run produced a deployed model. Caching skips steps whose inputs are unchanged, and you can set task-level retries. It is the backbone for reproducible training and retraining.
fj: Vertex AI Pipelines requires you to run and manage a Kubeflow cluster on GKE.
fs: It's serverless and managed — you write KFP components but Google runs the control plane and execution, billed per run.

q: What is the Vertex AI Feature Store, and what problem does it solve?
a: Feature Store is a managed system for defining, storing, and serving ML features consistently for both training and inference. The current Feature Store uses BigQuery as the offline source of truth and serves low-latency online reads from optimized online-serving resources, so a feature is defined once and reused. Its core value is preventing training-serving skew: the same transformation logic and values feed your training set and your live predictions, instead of two code paths drifting apart. It also enables feature reuse and discovery across teams and point-in-time correct historical lookups for training. You register feature views over BigQuery tables and sync them online.
fj: A Feature Store is just a cache in front of your database.
fs: Its real job is consistency — the same feature definition serves training and online inference, killing training-serving skew.

q: What is Vertex AI Vector Search, and how does it fit a RAG pipeline?
a: Vector Search (formerly Matching Engine) is a high-scale, low-latency vector database that finds nearest-neighbor embeddings using Google's ScaNN algorithm — the same tech behind Search and YouTube. In RAG it owns the retrieval step: you embed your documents, build an index, and at query time embed the question and fetch the top-k most similar chunks to ground the LLM. You build an ANN (approximate) index for production scale; a brute-force index is only for measuring recall (ground truth), not serving. It scales to billions of vectors but is a component, not an end-to-end RAG solution — you still own chunking, embedding, and prompt assembly.
fj: Vector Search is a full RAG system that handles chunking and prompting for you.
fs: It only does fast nearest-neighbor retrieval — you still own chunking, embedding, and assembling the grounded prompt.

q: Which embedding model do you reach for on Vertex AI, and what decisions matter?
a: Vertex offers managed text embedding models such as gemini-embedding-001 and text-embedding-005, plus multimodal embeddings for image and video. The key decisions are dimensionality (higher dimensions can mean better quality but larger index and cost), the task_type hint (RETRIEVAL_DOCUMENT vs RETRIEVAL_QUERY vs SEMANTIC_SIMILARITY, which conditions the vectors for your use case), and keeping the same model and version for indexing and querying so vectors stay comparable. You batch documents to control cost and respect token limits per input. Re-embedding everything is required if you change the model, so version pinning matters.
fj: You can embed documents with one model and queries with another.
fs: Index and query embeddings must come from the same model/version (and right task_type) or distances are meaningless.

q: How does grounding work on Vertex AI, and what are your options to reduce hallucination?
a: Grounding connects a model's answer to a verifiable source so it cites facts instead of inventing them. Vertex offers grounding with Google Search (the model issues live searches and returns citations, with optional dynamic retrieval that only grounds when needed), and grounding with your own data via Vertex AI Search or the RAG Engine over your documents. For full control you build custom RAG with Vector Search and inject retrieved context yourself. Grounded responses come back with supporting attributions you can surface to users. The choice is managed convenience (Vertex AI Search) versus control and cost (DIY Vector Search RAG).
fj: Grounding means the model was retrained on your documents.
fs: Grounding retrieves and injects sources at inference time and returns citations — no weights change.

q: What is Vertex AI Search (grounding with your data), and when do you prefer it over building RAG yourself?
a: Vertex AI Search is a managed retrieval service: you point it at your documents (Cloud Storage, BigQuery, websites) and it handles parsing, chunking, embedding, indexing, and ranking, exposing a search API and a grounding source for Gemini. You prefer it when you want production RAG quality fast without owning the retrieval stack, and you accept less control over chunking and ranking. You build DIY RAG with Vector Search when you need custom chunking, hybrid search, bespoke re-ranking, or tight cost control at scale. The RAG Engine sits in between as a managed orchestration layer you can still customize. The trade is time-to-value versus control.
fj: Vertex AI Search and Vector Search are the same product.
fs: Vector Search is the low-level ANN engine; Vertex AI Search is the managed end-to-end retrieval app built on top.

q: What is Vertex AI Agent Builder / Agent Engine, and what does it manage for you?
a: Agent Builder is a suite for building, deploying, and governing AI agents; Agent Engine is the managed runtime that hosts an agent, handling sessions, scaling, and integration with tools, grounding, and frameworks like ADK or LangGraph. An agent couples a Gemini model with tools (function calling, code execution, retrieval) so it can plan, call APIs, and act over multiple turns. Agent Engine adds production concerns: managed deployment, a Memory Bank for long-term memory, bidirectional streaming, and Agent-to-Agent protocol support. The honest caveat is it is less mature than rolling your own with the raw Gemini API plus LangGraph — observability into intermediate reasoning and custom control flow are more limited.
fj: Agent Engine is just a chatbot UI builder.
fs: It's a managed agent runtime — sessions, tool orchestration, memory, scaling — not a front-end widget.

q: Explain how tool calling (function calling) works with Gemini, and where the boundary of responsibility sits.
a: You declare tools as function schemas (name, description, parameters) and pass them with the request; when the model decides a tool is needed it returns a structured function call instead of text. Your code executes the function, returns the result back into the conversation, and the model uses it to produce the final answer. The model never runs your code — it only proposes calls, so you own validation, auth, and side effects. Good descriptions and typed parameters drive reliable selection, and you can force or auto-select tool use via the config. This is the foundation under agents and grounding.
fj: Gemini executes the function you declare and returns its result.
fs: The model only emits a structured call; your application runs the function and feeds the result back.

code.label: Declare a tool, get a call

q: Compare supervised fine-tuning, distillation, and RAG/grounding. When do you pick each on Vertex AI?
a: Supervised fine-tuning (SFT) adapts a Gemini model to your task and style using labeled prompt-completion pairs via a managed tuning pipeline — use it for consistent format, tone, or narrow domain behavior that prompting cannot reliably achieve. Distillation trains a smaller, cheaper student model to mimic a larger teacher, cutting latency and cost while retaining much of the quality. RAG/grounding injects fresh, factual context at inference and is the right tool for knowledge that changes or must be cited — it does not teach behavior. The rule of thumb: prompt first, then RAG for knowledge, then fine-tune for behavior, then distill for efficiency. They compose: a fine-tuned model can still use RAG.
fj: Fine-tuning is how you give a model your company's latest documents.
fs: Fine-tuning teaches behavior, not facts — for current, citable knowledge use RAG/grounding.

q: Walk through running a supervised fine-tuning job for Gemini on Vertex AI.
a: You prepare a JSONL dataset of prompt-completion examples (typically hundreds to thousands of high-quality pairs) staged in Cloud Storage, then launch a managed tuning job specifying the base model, dataset, and hyperparameters like epochs and learning-rate multiplier. Vertex runs the tuning with parameter-efficient methods under the hood and produces a tuned model you deploy to an endpoint and call like any Gemini model. You hold out a validation set and use the evaluation service to confirm the tuned model actually beats the base on your metric before promoting. Data quality and coverage of edge cases matter far more than dataset size.
fj: More tuning data is always better, regardless of quality.
fs: A few hundred clean, representative examples usually beat thousands of noisy ones — quality dominates.

q: Online vs batch prediction on Vertex AI — how do you choose, and how does serving differ?
a: Online prediction deploys a Model to an Endpoint as a DeployedModel with provisioned compute and returns low-latency synchronous responses — use it for real-time, request-driven inference. Batch prediction is an asynchronous BatchPredictionJob that scores a large dataset from Cloud Storage or BigQuery and writes results back, with no always-on endpoint — use it for periodic scoring where latency does not matter and cost efficiency does. Online endpoints cost money while deployed even when idle; batch spins up, runs, and tears down. The driver is latency requirement and traffic shape: steady real-time traffic favors online, bulk periodic scoring favors batch.
fj: Batch prediction needs an always-on endpoint just like online.
fs: Batch is a job that provisions, scores the dataset, and tears down — no standing endpoint or idle cost.

q: How does autoscaling work for a Vertex AI online endpoint, and what's the tradeoff with min replicas?
a: On a DeployedModel you set min and max replica counts and a machine type; Vertex monitors utilization (CPU, or GPU duty cycle for accelerated models) and adds or removes replicas to track demand. Setting min replicas to one or more keeps warm capacity that avoids cold-start latency but bills continuously even at zero traffic. Scaling out is not instant — new replicas need to provision and load the model — so bursty traffic can still see latency spikes if max is too low or scaling lags. You size min for your latency floor and baseline load, and max for your peak, balancing cost against tail latency.
fj: Autoscaling means you never pay when there's no traffic.
fs: Min replicas stay warm and billed; only scaling above the floor is demand-driven, and scale-up has provisioning lag.

q: How do you do a safe canary or A/B rollout of a new model version on a Vertex AI endpoint?
a: Deploy the new model version as a second DeployedModel on the same Endpoint and use traffic splitting to route a small percentage (say 5 or 10 percent) to it while the rest stays on the current model. You monitor latency, errors, and business or quality metrics on the canary, then ramp the split toward 100 percent as confidence grows, or roll back instantly by shifting traffic to zero. Because both versions share one endpoint, clients are unchanged. This gives canary and A/B testing without DNS or client changes. Pair it with model monitoring to catch skew or drift before full promotion.
fj: To test a new model you must stand up a separate endpoint and repoint clients.
fs: Deploy both models to one endpoint and shift the traffic split — no client or endpoint changes needed.

q: What does the Vertex AI Model Registry give you, and how does it fit MLOps?
a: The Model Registry is a central, versioned catalog of your models where each model can have multiple versions with aliases (like 'default' or 'production'), metadata, and links to the pipeline run and dataset that produced it. It is the handoff point between training and deployment: pipelines register a model version, you evaluate it, and you deploy a chosen alias to an endpoint, so promotions are explicit and auditable. Combined with ML Metadata lineage, you can trace any serving model back to its training run and data. It standardizes how versions are tracked and promoted across the team rather than passing model files around.
fj: The Model Registry is just a bucket where you copy model files.
fs: It's a versioned catalog with aliases, lineage, and evaluation links that drives controlled promotion to endpoints.

q: Explain training-serving skew vs prediction drift, and how Vertex AI Model Monitoring detects them.
a: Training-serving skew is when the feature distribution in production differs from the training data, often due to inconsistent preprocessing between paths; prediction drift is when recent production input distributions shift away from earlier production traffic over time. Model Monitoring logs incoming prediction requests to BigQuery and computes a statistical distance between distributions — Jensen-Shannon divergence for numerical features and L-infinity distance for categorical features — alerting when it crosses a threshold. Skew detection needs the original training data as a baseline; drift detection compares production against earlier production when you lack the training set. Both catch silent model degradation that accuracy alone would miss until too late.
fj: Skew and drift are the same thing measured against the same baseline.
fs: Skew compares production to training data; drift compares recent production to earlier production over time.

q: What is the Gen AI evaluation service, and why isn't accuracy enough for LLM outputs?
a: The Gen AI evaluation service measures generative model and agent quality using metrics suited to open-ended output: computation-based metrics (BLEU, ROUGE, exact match) and model-based 'autorater' metrics where a judge model scores responses for coherence, groundedness, safety, or instruction-following against a rubric. Unlike a single accuracy number, LLM tasks have many acceptable answers, so you need pairwise comparisons (is A better than B), pointwise rubrics, and reference-free checks like faithfulness to retrieved context. You run it on a held-out eval set before promoting a tuned model or new prompt, and it now supports agent evaluation and partner models. It turns 'looks good' into a measurable, repeatable gate.
fj: You can judge an LLM's output quality with a single accuracy metric like classification.
fs: Open-ended outputs need autorater/pairwise/rubric metrics (groundedness, coherence) — one accuracy number doesn't fit.

q: How do Vertex AI safety filters and responsible-AI controls work for Gemini, and where do they fall short?
a: Gemini on Vertex applies two layers: non-configurable filters that always block prohibited content like CSAM and certain PII, and configurable content filters across four harm categories (hate speech, harassment, sexually explicit, dangerous content) where you set block thresholds based on probability and severity (NEGLIGIBLE/LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH). You also steer behavior with system instructions and can inspect safety ratings on each response. Limits: filters are probabilistic and category-bound, so they miss domain-specific harms, prompt-injection, and jailbreaks — you still need your own input/output validation, grounding for factuality, and human review for high-stakes use. Safety settings reduce risk; they don't remove the need for application-level guardrails.
fj: Turning on Vertex safety filters makes the application fully safe.
fs: They're probabilistic and category-bound — you still need grounding, injection defenses, and app-level guardrails.

q: When should a team use managed Vertex AI GenAI versus building its own stack on raw infrastructure?
a: Choose Vertex when you value managed scaling, security, data residency, IAM-integrated governance, and a single platform spanning tuning, grounding, serving, evaluation, and monitoring — it shortens time-to-production and keeps data in your Google Cloud boundary. Build your own (vLLM/open weights on GKE, a self-hosted vector DB, custom orchestration) when you need a specific model not offered, extreme cost control at very high volume, on-prem or multi-cloud portability, or control Vertex's managed abstractions don't expose. The honest tradeoff: Vertex trades some flexibility and per-call cost for far less operational burden and built-in governance. Most teams start managed and only peel off the pieces where DIY clearly pays.
fj: Self-hosting open models is always cheaper than using Vertex.
fs: DIY only wins at high volume or for needs Vertex can't meet — it adds real ops, scaling, and governance work you'd otherwise get managed.

q: What cost and quota traps bite teams on Vertex AI, and how do you control them?
a: The big ones: idle online endpoints with min replicas billing 24/7 even at zero traffic; oversized Vector Search index serving nodes for low query volume; large-context Gemini calls where input tokens dominate cost; and re-running pipeline steps that caching could skip. GenAI calls are billed per token (input plus output), so context bloat and chatty agents add up fast. Region-level quotas (queries per minute, GPU availability) can throttle you, so you request increases ahead of launch and design for graceful backoff. Controls: prefer batch over standing endpoints for periodic work, right-size replicas and index nodes, use Flash models where quality allows, cache prompts and pipeline steps, and trim retrieved context.
fj: GenAI cost is mostly the output tokens, so just cap max output length.
fs: Input tokens (long context, big RAG payloads) often dominate — trim retrieved context and prompts, not just output.
